# Choosing the Right Job for AI {#sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"But they are useless. They can only give you answers."*
>
> — Pablo Picasso, *The Paris Review* (1964)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Picasso was speaking about calculating machines. For an
architect, a computed answer matters only when available feedback can challenge
it and the architect responsible for the decision can decide what follows.
Search and reinforcement learning need measurable objectives. Critique and
repair need checks that can reject their output.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Generation, prediction, optimization, critique, repair, verification, explanation, and coordination are different jobs inside an AI-assisted architecture study. The appropriate job depends on the architecture task, available feedback, evidence burden, and cost of being wrong. This chapter shows how to choose a method for that job while keeping architectural decision authority outside the method.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What job should an AI method perform, what feedback does that job require, and
which decisions remain with the architect?
:::

The accelerator study developed later in @sec-running-the-loop provides a
concrete example. An AI method can propose legal array geometries, predict which
geometry might reduce cycles, choose the next configuration to evaluate, or
inspect the resulting evidence for a missing comparison. SCALE-Sim, the
cycle-level systolic-array simulator used in that study, can test the proposed
configurations. None of these jobs is interchangeable. A proposal needs
legality and tool checks, a prediction needs calibration, and a choice of the
next experiment needs a declared objective and budget.

@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces showed how an architecture
environment exposes legal actions, returns observations, and records failures.
Once those interfaces exist, choosing a method is a question of what work needs
to be done inside them. Current model rankings will age. The durable choice is
whether an architect needs more candidates, a cheaper estimate, a better use of
scarce tool runs, or a stronger review of existing evidence.

@sec-moonshot previewed six roles tied directly to candidates and checks:
generation, prediction, optimization, critique, repair, and verification. This
chapter also names explanation and coordination because tool-using systems must
connect results to proposed mechanisms and pass state among components. Those
two jobs cut across the six roles; neither grants a new decision right.

Capability and authority answer different questions. Capability asks what a
method can generate, estimate, check, repair, explain, or route. Authority asks
what the architect permits the method to do and which decisions remain outside
it. A successful tool call, prediction, or verification step demonstrates a
capability within its tested scope. It does not authorize a stronger
architecture decision.

A model that can generate plausible schedules,
configuration files, code fragments, RTL snippets, or design prose may be
useful for drafting alternatives, but weak for choosing among them. A
surrogate may predict latency or energy well inside a calibrated accelerator
design region, but fail outside the sampled space. Bayesian optimization[^fn-ml-bo-acquisition-policy] may
spend scarce simulator or synthesis evaluations carefully, but only if the
action space and objective are well formed. Reinforcement learning[^fn-rl-delayed-feedback] may be
attractive for placement, scheduling, or adaptive control, but dangerous when
invalid actions are common and feedback is delayed. A critic may be more
valuable than a generator when the workload was never documented, not when the
architect needs more candidates.

[^fn-ml-bo-acquisition-policy]: In this chapter, Bayesian optimization matters as the acquisition policy that chooses which scarce simulator or synthesis evaluation to spend next. Its value depends less on the method name than on a well-formed action space, objective, uncertainty model, and escalation rule.

[^fn-rl-delayed-feedback]: For these method roles, the RL issue is delayed and invalid feedback. Placement, scheduling, and adaptive-control loops can make RL attractive, while illegal moves or late rewards make the loop's rejection checks decisive.

> **Method role.** A method role is the specific job a model, search procedure,
> tool, script, or person performs inside a design loop, together with the state
> it may read, the actions it may take, the feedback it receives, and the
> authority its output does not exceed.

> **Feedback budget.** A feedback budget records the amount, latency, cost, and
> fidelity of evaluation available across tool runs, measurements, failed
> attempts, and review.

Generation proposes candidates. Prediction estimates candidate properties.
Optimization chooses what to evaluate next. Critique finds flaws, repair fixes
malformed artifacts, explanation relates results to proposed mechanisms, and
coordination routes state and work. Verification appears here only as a
checking role;
@sec-feedback-verification-trust covers the broader rejection and evidence
process.

An architect should choose a method according to the required job, available
feedback, and checks that can challenge its output, not according to an overall
model ranking. A reviewer can then ask what state the method saw, what actions
it took, and which result forced it to stop or return control to the architect.

Tensor-program autotuning gives a small version of this role split. AutoTVM,
introduced in @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales, and the follow-on
tensor-program optimizer Ansor generate candidate schedules, predict
or rank promising regions, spend measured hardware feedback selectively, and
reject candidates that fail correctness or performance checks
[@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM; @ZhengEtAl2020Ansor]. These autotuning systems connect
each job to measured feedback and rejection checks. Search alone does not
establish an architectural claim.

Machine learning for architecture did not begin with recent generative methods.
Regression models and learned surrogates predicted performance or power, while
predictive DSE and autotuners searched defined spaces inside methodologies whose
tasks, tools, workloads, and acceptance rules were specified by people
[@LeeBrooks2006RegressionModeling; @IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE;
@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM; @ZhengEtAl2020Ansor]. The perceptron branch predictor is
an instructive learned mechanism in this lineage [@JimenezLin2001Perceptron].
Its role sits at the cheap extreme of the feedback budget, where retirement
grades every prediction against ground truth within cycles and updates predictor
state from the outcome. Design-time feedback is far more expensive, so these
methods need an architecture study that states their actions, costs, and
rejection checks. In those examples, learning
operated inside the artifact or as a scoped predictor or optimizer within a
human-specified methodology. Current systems can also generate or edit
artifacts, call tools, and choose experiments when an environment provides
those actions [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym; @OuyangEtAl2025KernelBench;
@BlockloveEtAl2023ChipChat]. These capabilities automate more of the work
leading to a reviewed claim. The design-loop card introduced in
@sec-architecture-20-ontology records the assigned role, available feedback,
and authority limit. @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives its complete schema
and rubric.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can choose an AI method for an architecture task and
state the limits of its role.
That means you can:

- **Match** generation, prediction, optimization, critique, repair, verification,
  explanation, or coordination to the architecture work that needs to be done;
- **Separate** what a method can do from the decisions that remain with the
  architect;
- **Allocate** scarce feedback among proposing, estimating, searching, checking, and repairing;
- **Defend** a method choice in terms of its object, feedback, rejection path, and authority limit.
:::

## Match the Method to the Architecture Task

Start with the architecture work that needs help. Design-space exploration,
workload characterization, benchmark construction, code generation, RTL
repair, compiler/runtime tuning, accelerator search, chiplet partitioning,
physical-design assistance, and evidence critique are not the same problem.
They require different state, allow different actions, tolerate different
errors, and require different feedback.

ArchGym, the worked example in
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces, makes method comparisons more
meaningful by defining tasks, actions, observations, workloads, and feedback
[@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. A shared environment still does not decide which
job an AI method should perform. An architect who already has too many
candidates needs better ranking or critique, not another generator. An
architect with only a few expensive synthesis runs may need an optimizer that
chooses where to spend them.

@fig-method-role-map connects that method choice to the rest of a bounded
architecture study. Read it from the study conditions on the left, through the
jobs and outputs in the center, to review by the architect or a named reviewer
on the right. No method output goes directly to a design decision.

![**Method roles in AI-assisted architecture work.** Generation, prediction, optimization, critique, repair, verification, explanation, and coordination each need recorded state, feedback, rejection criteria, and a decision boundary. Their outputs go to the architect or named reviewer responsible for accepting, rejecting, escalating, or revising the claim.](images/F7-method-role-map){#fig-method-role-map width="100%" fig-alt="Role map showing generation, prediction, optimization, critique, repair, verification, explanation, and coordination as defined roles inside an architecture project, with their outputs feeding review by the architect or a named reviewer."}

The left side of @fig-method-role-map supplies the conditions for choosing a
role. The task and claim state what the architect wants to learn. The
representation determines what state the method can see, while the environment
determines which actions and observations are available. The feedback budget
and constraints limit the experiments the method can request. The decision
boundary sets the strongest action its output may support.

The center shows the distinct jobs. Generation returns candidates, prediction
returns estimates, and optimization selects the next experiment. Critique
returns findings, while repair returns revisions. Verification returns check
results, explanation develops a mechanism account, and coordination routes
state and work among the roles. A project may use only one role or combine
several. For
example, a generator may propose array geometries, a predictor may rank them,
and independent tool checks may reject illegal or incorrect candidates.

All of those outputs go to the architect or a named reviewer on the
right. That reviewer can accept, reject, revise, or escalate the claim, but a
method output cannot take that step on its own. @tbl-method-role-limits makes
the difference concrete by pairing each permitted action with the feedback or
check that can challenge it and the decision it cannot make.

| **Role** | **Permitted action** | **Feedback or check** | **Authority limit** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Generate | Propose configurations, code, RTL fragments, tests, reports, or hypotheses. | Parsers, compilers, tests, constraints, provenance, and review. | A valid proposal is not an accepted design. |
| Predict | Estimate performance, energy, area, latency, reliability, or cost. | Calibration, support region, uncertainty, held-out checks, and counterexamples. | A calibrated estimate can inform ranking or escalation only inside its tested scope. |
| Optimize | Choose the next candidate, region, or experiment to evaluate. | Declared objective, legal actions, cost model, feedback budget, and stopping rule. | A sampling policy does not own the resulting architecture decision. |
| Critique | Find weak assumptions, missing evidence, unsupported claims, or invalid actions. | Artifact provenance, comparison baseline, counterexamples, and unresolved failures. | A critique may trigger rejection or escalation only through the declared review policy. |
| Repair | Propose corrections to malformed artifacts. | Original artifact, patch or diff, rerun checks, and remaining failures. | Repair may correct form; it may not waive a violated design constraint. |
| Verify | Check invariants, tests, tool outputs, and evidence records. | Reproducible checks, provenance, independence basis, and escalation rules. | A passing check supports only the property and scope it tested. |
| Explain | Develop a mechanism account and state tradeoffs, failures, uncertainty, and rejected alternatives clearly. | Traceable results, assumptions, contrast cases, and stated limits. | A persuasive account cannot make weak results support a stronger claim. |
| Coordinate | Route state, tasks, tool calls, and evidence among agents, tools, and reviewers. | Shared state, actor permissions, versioned records, and stop or escalation rules. | Routing work cannot silently expand or combine decision authority. |

: **Each method role needs different feedback and checks:** Generation, prediction, optimization, critique, repair, verification, explanation, and coordination produce different outputs and require different ways to challenge them. {#tbl-method-role-limits tbl-colwidths="[18,28,25,19]"}

Read across a row rather than treating the roles as a ladder. A predictor may
rank candidates inside its calibrated range, but the calibration evidence does
not authorize a final power or timing claim. A repair method may fix malformed
RTL, but passing syntax checks does not waive a failed timing constraint. The
last column keeps the demonstrated capability narrower than the architectural
decision.

::: {.callout-engineer-move title="State every AI method claim concretely"}
For every method, state the artifact or parameter it changes, the interface it
uses, the feedback it receives, the check that can reject its output, and the
person responsible for the resulting decision. A concrete sentence might read,
"The optimizer proposes array geometries, SCALE-Sim evaluates them, and the
responsible architect decides whether any candidate warrants further analysis."
:::

The object being changed and the cost of a mistake also matter. Generating a
benchmark question and generating an RTL block use the same broad capability,
but the RTL change needs stronger tool feedback before anyone relies on it.
@tbl-method-object-authority shows how the permitted action stays narrower than
the decision it may inform.

| **Capability** | **Permitted within the role** | **Does not authorize** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Generate | Draft benchmark questions, where editorial review is check enough, or RTL candidates, where physical-design consequence demands tool rejection before ranking. | Treating either draft as a verified artifact or architecture decision. |
| Predict | Rank candidates or trigger escalation inside a calibrated support region. | Claiming final power, timing, reliability, or deployment behavior. |
| Optimize | Select the next proxy, simulation, synthesis, or measurement to spend. | Selecting the winning candidate for implementation or changing the declared objective. |

: **The object and consequence bound method authority:** The same capability may draft, rank, or route within scope without acquiring authority to verify a result, approve a waiver, or make the final decision. {#tbl-method-object-authority tbl-colwidths="[24,36,30]"}

The same action can be safe when it only creates a proposal and unsafe when it
changes a design without approval. Method selection must account for the
artifact and consequence, not only the model's demonstrated capability.

## What Hardware Awareness Requires

Before assigning a method a larger role, an architect needs to know whether it
can act on real hardware constraints or merely repeat hardware terms. A kernel
generator may mention caches, vector units, power targets, and a process node
yet produce code that violates memory limits or performs poorly on the target.
The useful test is what hardware-relevant action the method can take and what
tool result or measurement can reject it.

> **Hardware awareness.** Here, a method is hardware aware only to the extent
> that it respects hardware-relevant constraints, acts within a legal
> hardware/software design space, and uses feedback from appropriate tools or
> measurements. The architect separately decides what claim that feedback can
> support and what the method may do next.

Methods increasingly generate or revise kernels, compiler settings,
accelerator configurations, RTL fragments, placement constraints, and design
reports. Hardware-aware neural architecture search[^fn-ml-nas], in the ML
model-design sense, shows one way to put latency, energy, memory, and device
cost inside a search problem [@BenmezianeEtAl2021HardwareAwareNAS]. For
architecture work, the label is useful only when the constraints, legal
actions, and feedback path are named.

[^fn-ml-nas]: Neural architecture search (NAS) automates the design of neural network architectures. This chapter uses it as a cautionary adjacent field. Its results can be interpreted only when the search space, data, training budget, evaluation rule, and rejected alternatives are recorded.

*Hardware aware* can hide several different claims. A model may use the right
vocabulary yet ignore a memory limit. A surrogate may predict performance
without being able to run the relevant tool. A tool-using method may compile
and profile an artifact without having a calibrated model outside the tested
configurations. The label alone therefore says little about which job the
method can perform safely.

The proxy-mismatch problem from @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces
also constrains any method that optimizes the proxy. When a method leaves its
calibrated range or encounters a constraint it cannot resolve, the harness
should stop it and return the result to the named architect.
@sec-feedback-verification-trust explains how the architect decides whether to
reject the result, seek stronger evidence, or escalate.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Stop when the evidence runs out"}
Record the uncertainty, failed runs, and rejected alternatives that caused the
method to stop. Those records help the architect decide whether to request
stronger feedback, revise the claim, or end the work. They do not authorize the
method to waive a constraint or make the final decision.
:::

Functional correctness is the baseline whenever a method changes an
executable or synthesizable artifact. Tests, reference outputs, formal checks,
type or interface checks, synthesis constraints, numerical tolerances, or
expert review must be able to reject it before performance claims matter.

Before choosing a method, the architect should identify which hardware
capability the job actually requires. @tbl-hardware-awareness-assessment
separates three claims often compressed into *hardware aware*. The rows are not
a maturity ladder, and a method may satisfy one without satisfying the others.

| **Required capability** | **What the method may do** | **Feedback needed** | **What remains with the architect** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Constraints and legal actions | Generate or change candidate fields within declared hardware and software limits. | Bounds, static checks, invalid-action filters, and feasibility rules. | Change or waive the declared constraints. |
| Performance prediction | Estimate or rank behavior inside a calibrated support region. | Sensitivity, residuals, held-out error, and checks outside the fitted region. | Decide whether the estimate is adequate for the proposed next step. |
| Tool-grounded artifacts | Generate or revise code, configurations, kernels, traces, or RTL that a relevant tool can check. | Compile, run, profile, simulate, synthesize, and preserve both successful and failed outcomes. | Interpret what the tool result supports and authorize what happens next. |

: **Name the hardware capability the job requires.** Constraints, calibrated predictions, and tool-grounded artifacts require different feedback and leave different decisions with the architect. {#tbl-hardware-awareness-assessment tbl-colwidths="[20,28,27,25]"}

Calling a generator "hardware aware" does not establish which row applies. A
kernel generator that can compile, test, and profile has demonstrated
tool-grounded action. A reviewer still needs the correctness tests, portability
results, calibration data, failed cases, and the decision those results were
used to support.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Generation: Proposing Candidates and Artifacts

Generation helps when an architect needs more alternatives or a faster first
draft. An AI method can propose a simulator configuration, generate code or an
RTL fragment, produce a test bench, suggest a memory hierarchy, or enumerate
hypotheses about a workload. Fluent artifacts make this role easy to
demonstrate and easy to overtrust.

In the bounded accelerator study in @sec-running-the-loop, generation proposes
three legal array geometries, qualitative behavior, a failure mode, and a
directional mechanism that can be tested. Schema and semantic checks establish
that the response is a legal proposal within the declared constraints. They do
not establish that the proposal is faster or that its mechanism is correct.

That distinction holds for any generated artifact. A syntactically valid RTL
fragment may still fail simulation, routing, or timing closure. Before an
architect relies on it, the artifact needs validity checks, tool execution, a
matched baseline, retained failures, and review by the person responsible for
the decision.

Candidate generation also extends beyond writing direct code. For spatial
architectures such as tensor accelerators, a generator must represent and
manipulate *dataflow*, the spatial and temporal unrolling of operations, as a
primary structural contract rather than a scalar hyperparameter. That choice
changes reuse, memory traffic, interconnect demand, and which tool feedback can
reject the proposal. It also supplies a mechanism the architect can test.

::: {.callout-failure-mode title="Treating AI generation as design"}
It is tempting to treat a plausible AI-generated artifact, such as an RTL fragment,
a configuration, or a benchmark question, as a result. It is a proposal. Tool
checks, a matched baseline, and retained failures determine what the proposal
supports. The architect responsible for the decision determines whether that
evidence justifies further work or the proposed action.
:::

Generation is especially useful for breadth. It can propose
candidate decompositions, list assumptions, create alternative experiment
plans, translate design intent into structured records, or draft the first
version of a design-loop card. @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives the full
card and rubric. These outputs give the architect more structured material to
inspect. They remain proposals until an appropriate check evaluates them.

Kernel generation makes the separation visible because a compute subsystem
depends on target-specific kernels that are both correct and efficient.
KernelBench, an evaluation framework for AI-generated code, asks language
models to produce GPU kernels for PyTorch workloads
[@OuyangEtAl2025KernelBench]. Its harness compiles each candidate, checks
numerical correctness, and compares performance with the PyTorch Eager
baseline.

@fig-ch06-kernel-funnel shows the outcome of one attempt per task for four
models across three difficulty levels. The dashed line marks attempted tasks,
the blue bars mark correct kernels, and the green bars mark kernels that are
both correct and faster than the baseline. The important relationship is the
drop between those three conditions. Generating code, producing correct code,
and improving performance are separate achievements.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch06-kernel-funnel
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Attempted is not correct, and correct is not fast:** One-shot KernelBench results for four language models across the benchmark's three difficulty levels, measured on an NVIDIA L40S GPU [@OuyangEtAl2025KernelBench]. The dashed 100 percent line marks tasks attempted, not measured fluency or plausibility. The harness's correctness oracle passes far fewer candidates, and only a small fraction are both correct and faster than the PyTorch Eager baseline. The paper prints no per-model compile rates, and these panels chart none of them; in the paper's separate 100-kernel error-analysis sample, 83 of 100 generated kernels compile and 73 execute before the correctness oracle prunes the set to 50. Values transcribed from the paper's published single-attempt tables.
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Three-panel grouped bar chart of one-shot KernelBench outcomes for Llama-3.1-70B, DeepSeek V3, OpenAI o1, and DeepSeek R1 at difficulty levels one, two, and three. In each panel a dashed line at one hundred percent marks tasks attempted; blue bars show the share passing the correctness oracle, from zero to sixty-seven percent, and green bars show the share both correct and faster than the PyTorch Eager baseline, from zero to thirty-six percent. Both bars sit far below the dashed line everywhere, and the fast share is always well below the correct share."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter6-kernelbench-funnel.csv
# KernelBench [@OuyangEtAl2025KernelBench], single greedy attempt on NVIDIA
# L40S: one-shot correctness (fast_0, Tables 8 and 9) and correct-and-faster-
# than-PyTorch-Eager (fast_1, Tables 1 and 9), percent of tasks per level.
# Per-model compile/execution rates are not printed in the paper (its Figure 3
# is unlabeled), so no compile-stage bars; the source record includes the paper's
# 100-kernel supplementary sample (83 compile / 73 execute / 50 correct) as
# context only.
models = ["Llama\n3.1-70B", "DeepSeek\nV3", "OpenAI\no1", "DeepSeek\nR1"]
correct = {1: [26, 43, 55, 67], 2: [0, 6, 56, 62], 3: [0, 30, 56, 8]}
fast1 = {1: [3, 6, 10, 12], 2: [0, 4, 24, 36], 3: [0, 8, 12, 2]}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(5.15, 1.95), sharey=True)
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.09, right=0.985, top=0.80, bottom=0.24, wspace=0.10)

width = 0.36
xs = range(len(models))
for ax, level in zip(axes, (1, 2, 3)):
    ax.axhline(100, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)),
               zorder=1)
    ax.bar([x - width / 2 for x in xs], correct[level], width=width * 0.94,
           color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=2,
           label="correct" if level == 1 else None)
    ax.bar([x + width / 2 for x in xs], fast1[level], width=width * 0.94,
           color=COLORS["evidence"], zorder=2,
           label="correct and faster\nthan baseline" if level == 1 else None)
    ax.set_title(f"Level {level}", fontsize=6.6, pad=3)
    ax.set_xticks(list(xs))
    ax.set_xticklabels(models, fontsize=5.1)
    ax.set_ylim(0, 112)
    ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
    ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55)
    for spine in ["top", "right"]:
        ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
    ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
axes[0].set_ylabel("share of tasks (%)", fontsize=6.4)
axes[0].annotate("task attempted", (0.05, 100), textcoords="offset points",
                 xytext=(0, 3), fontsize=5.4, color=COLORS["muted"])
fig.legend(loc="upper center", ncol=2, fontsize=6, frameon=False,
           bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, 1.0), handlelength=1.0, columnspacing=1.4)
```
Across every panel in @fig-ch06-kernel-funnel, the correct share sits well below
the attempted share, and the correct-and-faster share is smaller again. The
model ordering also changes with task difficulty. An architect cannot infer
kernel quality from fluency or from an overall model ranking. The generator
proposes code, while the harness determines correctness and the baseline
comparison determines whether the code is faster under the tested conditions.

A reusable KernelBench-style evaluation records the target platform,
correctness oracle, numerical tolerance, baseline, failed kernels, portability
limits, and the rejection rule for fast but incorrect code. Follow-on
kernel-generation benchmarks extend those checks to multiple backends and
portability contracts [@WenEtAl2025MultiKernelBench]. Category-aware analyses
also show that correctness, task structure, numerical contracts, and efficiency
can diverge [@WangEtAl2026KernelBenchX]. When proposals already arrive faster
than they can be rejected (@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales), generating more
only lengthens the backlog unless checking capacity also improves.

Chip-Chat brings the same role separation into RTL design. In that interactive
hardware design study, a language model drafts Verilog, open-source simulation
and synthesis tools return errors for revision, and a small processor design
proceeds as far as fabrication [@BlockloveEtAl2023ChipChat;
@ThakurEtAl2023VeriGen; @HeEtAl2024LLM4EDA]. The generator supplied drafts. A
parser, simulator, and synthesis flow could reject them, while a human designer
decided when a candidate was ready to advance. Another engineer could
review the result because those responsibilities remained separate.

## Prediction: Estimating Behavior Before Full Evaluation {#sec-prediction-estimating-behavior-before-full-evaluation}

Prediction helps an architect avoid a full tool run for every candidate. Long
before recent foundation models,[^fn-ml-foundation-models] architects used
statistical and machine-learning models to reduce the cost of exploring large
design spaces. Regression models for microarchitectural performance and power,
and predictive models for architectural design-space exploration, are part of
this lineage [@LeeBrooks2006RegressionModeling; @IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. A
predictor can guide an architecture decision only when its support region,
uncertainty, calibration source, escalation trigger, and decision owner are
stated.

[^fn-ml-foundation-models]: Recent foundation models changed expectations about prediction, but architecture's regression models and learned surrogates for design-space exploration predate them. AI-assisted studies should preserve those calibration practices.

The prediction role is not limited to performance. A predictor might estimate
energy, area, latency, reliability, queueing behavior, memory traffic,
thermal behavior, compile time, implementation feasibility, or deployment
impact. It might be a regression model, a learned surrogate, a calibrated
analytic model, a simulator-backed approximation, or a hybrid that combines
domain structure with data.

The estimate is only as useful as the feedback behind it. A latency predictor
can score an accelerator matrix unit without observing that its SRAM banking
causes congestion or breaks timing after place and route. A single-kernel
latency may have a clean calibration target, while deployment impact combines
power, thermal behavior, reliability, and changing workloads. The predictor
can supply an estimate that a declared policy uses to prune, defer, or request
stronger feedback. It cannot set that policy or authorize the resulting
action.

For every estimate, the architect should know which workloads and design
regions supplied the calibration data, whether the candidate changes memory
behavior, vector width, or technology assumptions, and which higher-fidelity
result can test the estimate. Those details show whether the prediction is an
interpolation inside known support or an extrapolation that needs escalation.

Conformal prediction[^fn-stats-conformal-prediction] provides one rigorous way
to attach a calibrated interval to a surrogate when its assumptions apply. The
interval has marginal coverage at a chosen probability under an exchangeability
assumption[^fn-stats-exchangeability] and a valid calibration set and
nonconformity score [@AngelopoulosBates2021Conformal]. Architecture workloads,
tools, and process corners may violate exchangeability. The guarantee is
marginal, not a promise of coverage in every workload region or design-space
pocket. Conformal coverage does not require a particular error distribution for
the surrogate, but it still depends on a meaningful calibration set and the
exchangeability assumption. The run record should preserve the interval,
calibration data, and support region.

@fig-ch06-conformal-coverage isolates the failure mode with synthetic data. On
the left, observations come from the calibrated support region and mostly stay
inside the nominal 90 percent band. On the right, the observed behavior shifts
while the surrogate and band continue unchanged. The relationship to inspect is
the growing separation between the red observations and the fixed band.

[^fn-stats-conformal-prediction]: Conformal prediction is a statistical technique that produces prediction sets or intervals from a model, with finite-sample coverage guarantees under an exchangeability assumption. Here, a calibrated interval may be used to prune, defer, or escalate a candidate, but it does not authorize a design decision.

[^fn-stats-exchangeability]: Exchangeability is a statistical property where the joint distribution of a sequence of random variables is invariant to their permutation, a weaker assumption than being independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.). The chapter stresses it because architecture workloads, tools, and process corners are often not exchangeable, so the conformal interval must be audited rather than trusted.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch06-conformal-coverage
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **A conformal interval is a diagnostic, not a safety promise:** Constructed illustration of a split-conformal band around a latency-like surrogate. Inside the calibrated support region, empirical coverage matches the nominal 90% level; under distribution shift the band keeps its width while coverage collapses, so the interval should trigger scrutiny and escalation rather than certify the proxy. All values are synthetic and illustrative, not measurements of any real system.
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Scatter plot of synthetic latency-like data with a fixed-width conformal prediction band around a surrogate curve. In the calibrated support region on the left, nearly all points fall inside the band; in the distribution-shift region on the right, observed behavior rises away from the unchanged band and most points escape it, showing coverage collapse."

import math
import random
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Constructed illustration: every value is synthetic, generated in this cell
# with a fixed seed; no real system was measured. The split-conformal recipe
# (calibration residual quantile -> fixed-width band) follows the conformal
# prediction pattern cited in the text.
rng = random.Random(20260713)
SHIFT_X = 6.0
NOISE = 0.32
X_MAX = 10.0

def surrogate(x):
    # The pattern the surrogate learned inside the calibrated region.
    return 4.0 + 1.8 * math.sin(0.9 * x) + 0.25 * x

def truth(x):
    # Beyond the calibrated region the workload regime changes.
    drift = 1.1 * (x - SHIFT_X) ** 1.25 if x > SHIFT_X else 0.0
    return surrogate(x) + drift

def observe(x):
    return truth(x) + rng.gauss(0.0, NOISE)

# Split conformal: the 90% quantile of absolute calibration residuals from the
# supported region sets a fixed band half-width q.
cal_x = [rng.uniform(0.0, SHIFT_X) for _ in range(60)]
cal_scores = sorted(abs(observe(x) - surrogate(x)) for x in cal_x)
q = cal_scores[math.ceil(0.9 * (len(cal_scores) + 1)) - 1]

test_in = [(x, observe(x)) for x in [0.15 + i * (SHIFT_X - 0.3) / 24 for i in range(25)]]
test_out = [(x, observe(x)) for x in [SHIFT_X + 0.15 + i * (X_MAX - SHIFT_X - 0.3) / 24 for i in range(25)]]
cov_in = sum(abs(y - surrogate(x)) <= q for x, y in test_in) / len(test_in)
cov_out = sum(abs(y - surrogate(x)) <= q for x, y in test_out) / len(test_out)

xs = [i * X_MAX / 400 for i in range(401)]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.9, 2.55))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.11, right=0.97, top=0.86, bottom=0.19)

ax.fill_between(xs, [surrogate(x) - q for x in xs], [surrogate(x) + q for x in xs],
                color=COLORS["blue"], alpha=0.16, linewidth=0, zorder=1)
ax.plot(xs, [surrogate(x) for x in xs], color=COLORS["blue"], linewidth=1.1, zorder=2)
ax.plot(xs, [truth(x) for x in xs], color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.9,
        linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=2)

ax.scatter([x for x, _ in test_in], [y for _, y in test_in], s=9,
           color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=3)
miss = [(x, y) for x, y in test_out if abs(y - surrogate(x)) > q]
hit = [(x, y) for x, y in test_out if abs(y - surrogate(x)) <= q]
ax.scatter([x for x, _ in hit], [y for _, y in hit], s=9, color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=3)
ax.scatter([x for x, _ in miss], [y for _, y in miss], s=11, color=COLORS["red"], zorder=3)

ax.axvline(SHIFT_X, color="#AAB4BF", linewidth=0.7, linestyle="--", zorder=1)
ymin, ymax = ax.get_ylim()
ax.set_ylim(ymin, ymax + 1.2)
ax.text(SHIFT_X / 2, ax.get_ylim()[1] - 0.35, "calibrated support", ha="center",
        va="top", fontsize=6.0, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["workload_ink"])
ax.text((SHIFT_X + X_MAX) / 2, ax.get_ylim()[1] - 0.35, "distribution shift",
        ha="center", va="top", fontsize=6.0, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["constraints_ink"])
ax.text(SHIFT_X / 2, ax.get_ylim()[1] - 1.15,
        f"empirical coverage ≈ {cov_in:.0%}\n(nominal 90%)", ha="center", va="top",
        fontsize=5.4, color=COLORS["workload_ink"])
ax.text((SHIFT_X + X_MAX) / 2, ax.get_ylim()[1] - 1.15,
        f"same band width,\ncoverage ≈ {cov_out:.0%}", ha="center", va="top",
        fontsize=5.4, color=COLORS["constraints_ink"])

ax.annotate("surrogate ± conformal band", (1.7, surrogate(1.7) + q),
            textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-2, 5), fontsize=5.6,
            color=COLORS["workload_ink"])
ax.annotate("observed behavior", (8.2, truth(8.2)), textcoords="offset points",
            xytext=(-58, 2), fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"])

ax.set_xlabel("workload / design parameter (synthetic units)", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("latency-like metric\n(synthetic units)", fontsize=7)
ax.set_xlim(0, X_MAX)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)
for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Nothing in the surrogate's own report changes at the shift boundary in
@fig-ch06-conformal-coverage. The band keeps its width and its nominal 90
percent claim; only the observed points climbing out of it reveal that
empirical coverage has collapsed. When observations begin to fall outside the
interval, the architect should stop using the surrogate for that region and
request a higher-fidelity evaluation. @sec-feedback-verification-trust explains
how to judge whether the stronger result is adequate evidence for the decision.

The accelerator study in @sec-running-the-loop keeps the same distinction. The
model's qualitative prediction and mechanism help select a test, but neither
counts as performance evidence. SCALE-Sim evaluates the candidates, and a
matched probe falsifies the proposed mechanism. A rough predictor may still be
enough to discard obviously bad candidates. A consequential claim needs
calibration and independent observations matched to the decision.

## Optimization: Learning the Design Space

Optimization answers which candidate or experiment deserves the next
evaluation. It is often described as finding the best point under an objective,
but an architect may need a different result. The useful outcome may be a
Pareto region,[^fn-math-pareto-region] a constraint boundary, a sensitivity, a
ruled-out class of candidates, or a decision about which expensive experiment
to run next. Hardware design spaces are often discrete, non-differentiable, and
highly constrained, so an optimizer must work with sparse feedback and reject
illegal actions rather than assume a smooth search space.

[^fn-math-pareto-region]: A Pareto region here is the Pareto frontier introduced earlier, treated as one defensible target for architecture optimization under limited feedback, alongside a constraint boundary or a ruled-out class of candidates, rather than collapsing the search to a single best point.

Bayesian optimization was developed for sequential experiments on expensive
black-box functions [@JonesSchonlauWelch1998EGO;
@SnoekLarochelleAdams2012BayesianOptimization]. Its surrogate and acquisition
rule balance exploration against exploitation and make uncertainty part of the
choice of what to evaluate next. A surrogate plus an acquisition rule is useful
when architecture simulation, synthesis, or measurement runs are costly.
Recording the acquisition rule, score, and current uncertainty lets a reviewer
see why the optimizer selected a candidate. The run record should also include
evaluation fidelity, expected benefit, and the stopping condition. The
optimizer selects experiments. The architect decides what their results
justify.

One auditable acquisition rule is GP-UCB[^fn-ml-gp-ucb] (Gaussian Process Upper
Confidence Bound). It weighs a surrogate's predicted outcome against its
uncertainty and offers a cumulative-regret bound[^fn-ml-no-regret] under stated
kernel, smoothness, and noise assumptions. The logged acquisition rule, not the
bound alone, lets a reviewer examine why the optimizer selected a particular
point [@SrinivasEtAl2010GPUCB].

[^fn-ml-gp-ucb]: GP-UCB (Gaussian Process Upper Confidence Bound) is an acquisition function that selects the next point to evaluate by weighing both the expected reward and the uncertainty of a Gaussian Process model. Its value here is that the acquisition rule is auditable rather than ad hoc. The run record can show why uncertainty made a sample worth spending, while a reviewer checks the assumptions separately.

[^fn-ml-no-regret]: A no-regret guarantee is a formal promise about a sampling rule under stated assumptions, such as a suitable kernel, bounded noise, and smoothness of the objective. Under those assumptions, it bounds cumulative regret across the sampling sequence. The logged acquisition score still explains the particular choice.

Reinforcement learning can fit sequential problems such as placement,
scheduling, adaptive control, or multi-stage design flows. It is appropriate
only when the state, legal actions, transitions, and feedback match the
architecture task and the decision the evidence must support.

PrefixRL shows what a tightly defined design space makes possible. The
hardware-aware RL framework casts parallel-prefix arithmetic circuits, such as
adders, as a reinforcement-learning problem with logic synthesis in the
evaluation path [@RoyEtAl2021PrefixRL]. Each candidate comes from a declared
circuit space and receives a score from an actual synthesis run rather than a
hand-built proxy. A synthesis run can reject candidates because the action
space is restricted and the output is synthesizable.

That environment does not remove the need for a baseline. A learned optimizer
must still be compared with a well-tuned simpler method under the same action
space, tool flow, and evaluation budget. Added method complexity is not
evidence of an architectural advantage.

The public AlphaChip debate is useful here only as a reminder that learned and
classical methods need a shared evaluation. It does not settle whether
reinforcement learning is appropriate for placement.

::: {.callout-war-story title="A classical baseline remained competitive"}
**The claim.** AlphaChip drew attention because reinforcement learning was applied to macro
placement [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The gap.** In a later public assessment, a strong simulated-annealing baseline matched or beat the learned method on its
proxy cost for most modern test cases. The original authors disputed whether
that assessment reproduced the method and its intended setting
[@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** This does not establish that reinforcement learning is the wrong method for
placement. It shows that added method complexity does not establish an
advantage by itself. A stronger claim requires a shared evaluation showing
what the learned method improves over a well-tuned simpler baseline.
:::

The accelerator study in @sec-running-the-loop applies that matched-baseline
requirement. One model proposal competes with a deterministic aspect-ratio
heuristic under equal four-evaluation budgets. Both arms retain the same
baseline, and a held-out probe falsifies the model's mechanism account. The
architecture result, AI contribution, and explanation are evaluated
separately, so a tie cannot be reported as an AI advantage.

Matched baselines also matter in autotuning and compiler optimization.
AlphaDev's discovered sorting routines entered production only after passing
the same correctness harness applied to human-written code
[@MankowitzEtAl2023AlphaDev], and AutoPhase's high-level synthesis phase
orderings are scored by actual HLS runs [@HajAliEtAl2020AutoPhase]. These
results depend on correctness tests and actual tool runs, not model output
alone.

General program-autotuning frameworks such as OpenTuner
[@AnselEtAl2014OpenTuner], and tensor-program optimizers such as AutoTVM and
Ansor, use measured hardware feedback to correct learned cost models. The cost
model proposes promising schedules, the compiler builds the strongest
candidates, and hardware measurements update the cost model for the next round
[@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM; @ZhengEtAl2020Ansor]. These systems exercise
the compiler and runtime path through legal schedules, failed variants, and
measured feedback. A comparable architecture study should retain the candidate
schedule, compiler and runtime versions, measurement context, failed variants,
cost-model update, and the declared limit on what the measurement proves.
Floorplanning places more weight on high-consequence physical feedback, while
autotuning places more weight on the compiler and runtime path that makes a
specialized schedule usable.

A reviewer should judge an optimizer by what it reveals about the design space
and how it spends feedback, not only by its best score. Five questions make
that judgment concrete:

- Did it discover a robust region?
- Did it identify a proxy mismatch?
- Did it spend high-fidelity evaluations where they changed the decision?
- Did it preserve rejected alternatives?
- Did it show why one candidate was chosen over another?

If the run record cannot answer these questions, the optimizer may have
improved a score without improving architectural understanding.

## Spending Scarce Feedback {#sec-sample-efficiency-under-expensive-feedback}

In machine learning, sample efficiency usually describes how much performance
a method gains from a given number of examples or evaluations. Architecture
presents a related but broader engineering problem. Simulator, synthesis, and
hardware runs are scarce, and expert review also takes time. The architect must
therefore decide where each expensive evaluation goes. Failed runs and expert
rejections can be useful feedback records, but they are not all ML samples.

Architecture often has a few high-fidelity runs, more medium-fidelity
simulations, many cheap proxies, and hidden costs such as tool setup, debugging,
license availability, and review. The useful method role changes with that
budget.

Architecture design spaces hold orders of magnitude more candidates than any
real evaluation budget can reach. @fig-evidence-gap places representative
candidate counts and affordable feedback counts on one logarithmic axis. It
does not pair a particular design space with its own evaluation budget. The
rows above the divider include the 12,960-state slice from
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales, the accelerator design-space scale of
MAESTRO, AutoTVM-style operator tuning, and Timeloop's core mapspace expression
[@KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM;
@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop]. The rows below the divider reuse the illustrative
feedback budgets from @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces. Together,
the independent scale anchors show why a method must screen broadly and reserve
stronger feedback for a much smaller set of candidates.

```{python}
#| label: fig-evidence-gap
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Candidate spaces dwarf feasible evaluation budgets:** The upper and lower rows are independent scale anchors, not paired measurements. Their contrast explains why a method must screen broadly and reserve stronger feedback for a small set of candidates.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale display of representative design-space candidate counts above a divider and independent affordable-feedback ranges below it."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, draw_range_rows, top_log_axis

rows = [
    {"display_label": "12,960-state slice", "display_note": "before corners/workloads", "count_low": 12_960, "count_high": 12_960, "right_label": "~1.3e4", "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "MAESTRO accelerator DSE", "display_note": "reported candidate search scale", "count_low": 480_000_000, "count_high": 480_000_000, "right_label": "480M", "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "AutoTVM operator tuning", "display_note": "reported order-of-billions space", "count_low": 1_000_000_000, "count_high": 1_000_000_000, "right_label": "~1B", "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "Timeloop mapspace core", "display_note": "lower bound before factor choices", "count_low": 2_600_000_000_000_000_000, "count_high": 2_600_000_000_000_000_000, "right_label": ">2e18", "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "cheap proxy screening", "display_note": "representative affordable checks", "count_low": 100_000, "count_high": 1_000_000, "right_label": "1e5-1e6", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "cycle-level comparisons", "display_note": "representative scoped runs", "count_low": 10, "count_high": 100, "right_label": "10-100", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "RTL / physical feedback", "display_note": "scarce high-fidelity evaluations", "count_low": 3, "count_high": 20, "right_label": "3-20", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "silicon / deployment checks", "display_note": "few high-consequence evaluations", "count_low": 1, "count_high": 5, "right_label": "1-5", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
]

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.35, 2.95))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.38, right=0.85, top=0.82, bottom=0.27)

y_positions = [0.35, 1.35, 2.35, 3.35, 5.80, 6.80, 7.80, 8.80]
ax.set_ylim(-1.05, y_positions[-1] + 0.65)
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_yticks([])
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(0.8, 1e19),
    xticks=[1, 1e2, 1e4, 1e6, 1e9, 1e12, 1e15, 1e18],
    xticklabels=["1", r"$10^2$", r"$10^4$", r"$10^6$", r"$10^9$", r"$10^{12}$", r"$10^{15}$", r"$10^{18}$"],
    xlabel="count scale: candidates or affordable evaluations",
    tick_fontsize=6.0,
)

ax.axhline(4.45, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.65, linestyle="--", zorder=0)
ax.text(-0.74, -0.55, "design-space anchors", transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=5.8, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["methods_ink"], clip_on=False)
ax.text(-0.74, 5.10, "affordable feedback budgets", transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=5.8, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["note_text"], clip_on=False)

draw_range_rows(ax, rows, low_key="count_low", high_key="count_high", label_x=-0.68, right_x=1.04, label_fontsize=6.15, right_fontsize=5.85, y_positions=y_positions, show_notes=False)
add_note_box(fig, "High-fidelity tools can evaluate only a tiny slice\nof the plausible candidate space.", xywh=(0.12, 0.035, 0.76, 0.10), fontsize=5.4)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Because its rows are not paired, @fig-evidence-gap cannot say how many
evaluations a particular design space requires. It shows only why exhaustive
high-fidelity evaluation is usually infeasible. Cheap proxies may screen
thousands of candidates, while physical-design or silicon checks reach only a
few. An optimizer can rank or select candidates under the declared objective
and budget. The architect decides which uncertainty warrants stronger feedback
and which rejected regions matter to the claim.

The cost records described in @sec-data-representations-world-models can guide
that choice. A simulator result, warning, failed run, synthesis report,
measurement, or expert review can reduce uncertainty relevant to the decision.
One synthesis run that distinguishes the two leading candidates may be worth
more than another hundred proxy scores that preserve their ranking. The
architect should ask which uncertainty the next evaluation can resolve, whether
that result could change the candidate ranking or claim, and what the
evaluation will cost. Raw run count is not the objective.

The cost of obtaining feedback includes model inference, simulator or EDA tool
time, and the human effort required to inspect the output. Treating generation
as free hides both inference and review costs. When energy use or emissions
materially affect the comparison, the cost record should state how they were
measured or estimated.

Multi-fidelity Bayesian optimization[^fn-ml-multi-fidelity-bo] makes one part of
that choice explicit by selecting both a candidate and whether to use a cheap
proxy or an expensive simulation [@KandasamyEtAl2017MultiFidelityBO]. The run
record should preserve the acquisition rule, fidelity choice, assumptions,
returned result, rejected alternatives, and escalation threshold.

[^fn-ml-multi-fidelity-bo]: Multi-fidelity Bayesian optimization jointly chooses which design to evaluate and at which fidelity level, trading information gain against cost. That is the feedback-budget decision here, whether to spend a cheap proxy or an expensive simulation on a candidate. @sec-feedback-verification-trust explains what the different feedback levels can establish.

@tbl-sample-efficiency-regimes groups four common levels of feedback
availability. Read down the table as evaluation becomes more expensive and less
frequent. Cheap evaluations support broad screening. Expensive tools provide
observations that cheaper models cannot, so the method mix shifts toward
smaller candidate sets, stronger priors, critique, and explicit review. Cost
and fidelity do not alone determine whether a result is sufficient for a
decision. @sec-feedback-verification-trust explains what each feedback level
can establish.

| **Feedback availability** | **Typical setting** | **Method implication** | **What to preserve or decide** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Many cheap proxy runs | Analytic models, rough estimators, compiler hints. | Broad search, candidate generation, surrogate pretraining. | Record proxy limits and check for overfitting to the cheap metric. |
| Hundreds of simulations | Simulator-backed DSE or workload sweeps. | Bayesian optimization, active learning, transfer, sensitivity analysis. | Record seeds, configs, workloads, and failed runs. |
| Tens of expensive tool runs | Synthesis, physical design, emulation, or hardware-in-the-loop. | Strong priors, staged screening checks, human filtering, small candidate sets. | Record calibration and name who may reject a candidate or approve escalation. |
| Few high-consequence checks | Silicon, deployment, fleet experiments, or customer workloads. | Critique, evidence organization, conservative recommendations. | Name the decision owner and preserve the audit trail. |

: **Feedback availability shapes method choice:** Cheap proxies, moderate simulations, expensive EDA, and scarce high-consequence checks reward different mixtures of generation, prediction, optimization, critique, and verification. {#tbl-sample-efficiency-regimes tbl-colwidths="[18,24,26,22]"}

The progression in @tbl-sample-efficiency-regimes changes the job assigned to
AI. With cheap feedback, generation and broad search may help. With only a few
physical-design or deployment checks, organizing evidence and finding flaws may
be more useful than proposing another candidate. Failed simulations, invalid
candidates, timeouts, rejected configurations, and proxy mismatches mark the
boundary of the design space and should be retained.

@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack later examines how the complete architecture
study changes as evaluation cost, reversibility, and consequences change across
the stack.

The value of each evaluation also depends on what the environment records. If
it logs only final scores, the method and reviewer cannot reuse much. Workload
metadata, candidate structure, tool warnings, failure reasons, and fidelity
make the result more useful. @sec-data-representations-world-models and
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces explain how representation and
environment choices preserve that context.

## Critique, Repair, Explanation, and Coordination

An architect who already has hundreds of candidates and inconsistent run logs
does not need another proposed design. The more useful AI job may be to find a
missing assumption, check whether evidence matches a claim, compare
alternatives, repair a malformed artifact, or explain a tradeoff for review.

Critique can operate against existing human artifacts. A critic can inspect a
design-space report, simulator log,
configuration file, benchmark description, or paper draft. It can ask whether
the workload matches the claim, whether the metric is a proxy for the real
objective, whether rejected candidates are missing, whether tool versions are
recorded, or whether a table proves less than the prose claims.

Each criticism should point to the artifact, tool result, assumption, or missing
comparison it challenges. A relevant tool check or qualified reviewer then
tests the finding. The critic may request rejection or escalation, but producing
a persuasive criticism does not give it that authority.

Question-answering resources such as QuArch point toward one piece of this
problem, making architecture knowledge accessible to automated optimizers and reviewers
[@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch]. But critique requires more than answering
questions from papers. A critic also needs the study records that papers often
omit, including assumptions, tool settings, failed runs, rejected alternatives,
supporting evidence, and the decisions made from them.

Critique can save expensive evaluations as well as improve review. A critic
that catches a missing workload record, unsupported proxy claim, stale tool
version, or invalid action before a synthesis run may be more useful than
another generator. Adjacent code-review evidence shows both the opportunity
and the boundary. In one RLHF code setting,
model-written critiques caught more inserted bugs than the study's contractor
pool, while human-plus-critic teams wrote more comprehensive critiques than
contractors alone and hallucinated fewer problems than models alone
[@McAleeseEtAl2024CriticGPT]. That result motivates an assisted architecture
review role; it is not evidence that the same gain transfers without testing.
ML-assisted resolution of code-review comments runs at production scale at
Google [@FroemmgenEtAl2024CodeReview].

Repair methods address a narrower class of problems. They can correct a
malformed configuration, fix tool syntax, patch a test bench, regenerate a plot
with the right workload metadata, or clarify a design-loop card. A repair
method cannot waive a timing, power, interface, or signoff failure. Its
explanation should tell the architect what was malformed, what changed, which
checks were rerun, and what failure remains.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Constraint waivers"}
An automated method may repair malformed artifacts, but the repair role does
not include authority to relax a design constraint. The architect must record
the violation and obtain a waiver from the person authorized to accept that
risk before proceeding.
:::

Explanation uses recorded state, tool results, and rejected alternatives to
connect a result to the proposed architectural mechanism. In the accelerator
study, it should connect array geometry to utilization and cycle count and name
the matched probe that can falsify that account. A fluent explanation cannot
repair a failed probe or expand what the evidence supports. The architect
decides whether the explanation is adequate for the claim.

Coordination becomes useful when the work crosses several tools or method roles.
A coordinator might route a failed synthesis result to a repair method and then
request another run. To do that safely, it needs versioned shared state, stated
permissions, and stop or escalation rules. The architect sets those permissions
and decides what the rerun supports.

Verification appears here only as a checking role. @sec-feedback-verification-trust
develops the broader rejection authorities that determine whether evidence can
support a decision.

## Choosing a Method Under Constraints {#sec-choosing-a-method-under-constraints}

A method choice should be defensible in design review. The architect should be
able to name the task, the representation the method reads and writes, the
environment in which it acts, the available feedback, the check that can reject
its output, and the decision that remains with the architect.

@tbl-method-selection-matrix turns those conditions into a selection test. Read
each row from the question on the left to the appropriate response. A weak
condition narrows the method's role rather than becoming an assumption that the
method will somehow overcome.

| **Question** | **If yes** | **If no** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Are the task and authority limits stated? | Use stronger automation inside those limits. | First decompose the task or keep the method advisory. |
| Does the architect need more candidates? | Use generation for breadth, with validity checks attached. | Prefer critique, verification, evidence organization, or better rejection tests. |
| Can the proposed actions be checked? | Run the declared checks before evaluating or ranking candidates. | Keep the method advisory and require strict tool or human review. |
| Is feedback cheap enough? | Search, active learning, or online adaptation may be useful. | Use priors, surrogates, staged screening checks, and critique. |
| Is uncertainty reported? | Prediction can guide exploration. | Do not use a point estimate to support the claim. |
| Can the proposed action be reversed? | The architect may authorize automatic action within a declared, reversible scope. | Require stronger evidence and a named decision owner. |
| Is provenance recorded? | Claims can be traced and audited; a replay package can additionally support rerunning the work. | Do not make strong comparative claims. |

: **Method selection follows the task conditions.** The useful mix of method roles depends on action validity, feedback cost, fidelity, rollback cost, and rejection authority. {#tbl-method-selection-matrix tbl-colwidths="[20,31,31]"}

The table explains why one method can be appropriate in one project and
inappropriate in another. A generator may draft simulator configurations but
cannot authorize a physical-design change. A surrogate may rank early candidates
but cannot support a final power claim outside its calibration range. An RL
policy may control a reversible runtime setting while remaining advisory for a
high-consequence design decision. Favorable conditions may justify a larger
execution role. The architect still decides what the results support and
whether the work advances.

The accelerator study in @sec-running-the-loop follows this separation. The
model proposes candidates, a frozen heuristic supplies a fair comparison,
SCALE-Sim produces observations, and a matched probe tests the proposed
mechanism. The architect then decides what the evidence supports. Because the
jobs remain separate, a tie and a falsified explanation are still usable
results.

A method description should state the task, permitted actions, evaluation
budget, required evidence, and rejection checks in ordinary terms. Systems with
several agents or tools need the same clarity. Assigning roles to separate
components may improve throughput or specialization, but it does not strengthen
the claim by itself. For each component, record what it reads and writes and who
reviews its output.

::: {#pri-match-methods .callout-design-principle title="Choose the method for the bottleneck"}
Name the work limiting progress before choosing a method. Use generation when
the architect lacks candidates, prediction or optimization when expensive tool
runs are scarce, and critique when unreviewed evidence is accumulating. Add a
method only when you can state which work it reduces and which independent
check still settles the claim.
:::

## Open Research Questions

1. **How can runtime checks keep an AI method inside its assigned authority as conditions change?**
The role limits in @tbl-method-role-limits are easy to state and hard to
enforce when feedback cost, uncertainty, or consequence changes during a run.
Runtime checks need to detect when a predictor turns a scoped estimate into a
recommendation, or when a coordinator combines limited permissions into a
stronger action. They must distinguish real authority violations from harmless
handoffs and report the cost of doing so.

2. **How should an AI-assisted architecture project spend scarce feedback among method roles?**
Generation, prediction, critique, repair, and optimization consume feedback in
different ways. Under fixed simulator-hour, validation, latency, and
human-review budgets, the architect must decide when another candidate is more
useful than a refined predictor or a search for missing evidence. The
next-evaluation test in @sec-sample-efficiency-under-expensive-feedback asks
which uncertainty a new result could resolve, whether it could change the
ranking or claim, and what it would cost. Those choices do not transfer cleanly
across architecture tasks because tool costs and review loads differ. Any
allocation policy must decide when to switch among proposing a candidate,
refining a predictor, and seeking missing evidence.

3. **How can AI optimizers learn from failures without confusing their fidelity?**
Cheap warnings, proxy mismatches, synthesis timeouts, and high-fidelity failures
all carry useful information, but they do not support the same conclusion. An
optimizer must learn from this mixture without letting cheap negative evidence
masquerade as a physical-design or silicon rejection. The difficulty is
updating the search from all four signals while keeping each failure tied to
the fidelity and scope that produced it.

4. **What rejection suites can test hardware-aware AI generators?**
The architecture community lacks standardized tests designed to expose
unsupported predictions and invalid generative actions. A useful suite would
need intentionally invalid candidates, near misses, and valid candidates, plus
a clear statement of what passing the suite supports. It must cover enough
workloads, tools, and design conditions to make false passes and false
rejections meaningful without presenting itself as a universal hardware
oracle.

## Conclusion

An architect should choose an AI method by the job that needs to be done, the
feedback available for that job, and the evidence required to challenge its
output. Generation, prediction, optimization, critique, repair, verification,
explanation, and coordination support different claims. Demonstrating one of
those capabilities does not authorize the method to widen its scope, waive a
constraint, or make the final architecture decision.

Feedback cost changes which role is useful. Cheap checks can screen broad sets
of candidates, while expensive tool runs should be reserved for questions that
can change the decision. For every method, the architect should state its job,
feedback budget, rejection path, and authority limit. A method is useful when
it reduces uncertainty that matters to the decision, keeps the supporting
record visible, and leaves the decision with the architect.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Method choice is a role-and-budget decision, not a leaderboard. Capability inside a role does not grant broader authority.
- **Reader test:** For the current architecture task, can you name each method's job, feedback budget, rejection path, and authority limit?
- **Up next:** Once a method produces observations, @sec-feedback-verification-trust asks when those observations count as evidence and who may decide what happens next.
:::
